sn46.2:1.2: For the phrase “sustained by food” see DN 33:1.8.2, AN 10.27:10.5, AN 10.28:4.5, Kp 4:1.2 and notes. The word āhāra, literally “intake”, may be translated as “food” or “fuel” depending on context.
sn46.2:1.3: This passage can be seen as an expansion of the fourth satipaṭṭhāna, “observation of principles”, where the “principles” underlying the rise and fall of the hindrances and awakening factors are investigated. Similar teachings recur throughout this section, for example SN 46.51.
sn46.2:2.2: “Feature of beauty” is subhanimitta. In early Pali, nimitta is used for a feature or quality of the mind that, when focused on, promotes the growth of similar or related qualities. Thus focusing on beauty fosters the desire for that beauty. (See MN 5:6.1).
sn46.2:2.3: “Irrational” attention sees the beauty as something to be held on to and appropriated. The flaw lies not in the object, but in the response.
sn46.2:5.2: Worrying about anxiety makes the mind even more anxious.
sn46.2:6.2: Such as what the nature of the correct path (AN 3.65:3.8), the various wrong views (SN 24.1:5.1), the status of a saint after death (SN 44.9:5.2), or the reality of kamma (SN 42.13:21.2).
sn46.2:9.2: These include the meditations listed in the Satipaṭṭhana Sutta (MN 10).
sn46.2:10.2: Discrimination between good and evil is the foundation of wisdom. | The phrase sappaṭibhāgā, which appears here in a stock phrase, is given three mutually exclusive explanations in the commentary (see Bodhi’s note on this sutta). I think the commentary is influenced by the later sense of paṭibhāgā as a technical meditation term meaning “counterpart”. This sense is, however, absent from Sanskrit, where pratibhāga means “portion, share” (Rig Veda 8.99.3, Manu 8.307). The sense would then refer to individuals “with a portion” of dark or bright qualities. This is, in fact, the theme of the only other passage in Pali that uses this term (AN 6.62:10.2).
sn46.2:12.2: These are the things that bring wholesome joy to the mind, in and out of meditation.
sn46.2:14.2: The includes peaceful meditations such as mindfulness of breathing.
sn46.2:15.2: This includes the development of absorption, which is the basis for the equanimity of the fourth absorption, as well as the development of insight into attraction and repulsion (see MN 152).
